examples.title: Creating and Managing Users
examples.description: Common commands for user management in Linux
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Create new user with custom settings
useradd -u 1500 -g developers -G analysts -d /home/appuser -s /bin/bash -c "Application User" appuser

# Set password
passwd appuser

# Modify user (add to additional group)
usermod -aG docker appuser

# Lock/unlock account
usermod -L appuser # Lock
usermod -U appuser # Unlock

# Delete user
userdel -r appuser # Remove with home directory

# Display user info
id appuser
finger appuser
whoami
examples.explanation: These commands demonstrate practical user account management including creation with custom UID/GID, password setting, group membership modification, account locking/unlocking, and deletion with cleanup.

examples.title: Viewing Password File Entries
examples.description: Commands to examine /etc/passwd and related files
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# View specific user entry
grep "^root:" /etc/passwd

# List all users
cat /etc/passwd | cut -d: -f1

# Get UID for current user
id -u
id -un

# Get UID/GID info
id username

# View password shadow file (root only)
# cat /etc/shadow

# Check password status
passwd -S username
passwd -S -a

# Change user's login shell
chsh -s /bin/zsh username

# Display all groups
grep "^username:" /etc/group
examples.explanation: These commands help examine user account details, UID/GID information, password status, and group memberships from system files.

examples.title: User Login Authentication Algorithm
examples.description: Simplified algorithm for Linux user authentication
examples.code:
FUNCTION AuthenticateUser(username, password)
 // Step 1: Lookup user in /etc/passwd
 IF NOT FindEntry(username, "/etc/passwd") THEN
 RETURN Failure("User not found")
 END IF
 
 passwdEntry = GetPasswdEntry(username)
 uid = passwdEntry.uid
 gid = passwdEntry.gid
 shell = passwdEntry.shell
 home = passwdEntry.home
 
 // Step 2: Check account status via PAM
 IF NOT PAM_AccountCheck(username) THEN
 RETURN Failure("Account disabled")
 END IF
 
 // Step 3: Get password hash from /etc/shadow
 shadowEntry = GetShadowEntry(username)
 storedHash = shadowEntry.passwordHash
 
 // Step 4: Hash input password and compare
 inputHash = Hash(password, storedHash.algorithm)
 IF inputHash != storedHash THEN
 PAM_LogFailure(username)
 RETURN Failure("Invalid password")
 END IF
 
 // Step 5: Password aging checks
 IF IsPasswordExpired(shadowEntry) THEN
 RETURN Failure("Password expired")
 END IF
 
 // Step 6: Setup user session
 PAM_SessionOpen(username)
 SetEnvironment(uid, gid, home, shell)
 
 RETURN Success(shell)
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: This pseudocode represents the simplified user authentication flow in Linux, showing how /etc/passwd provides basic info while /etc/shadow provides secure password verification, with PAM handling additional security checks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) - Lookup operations are typically O(1) with proper indexing
